So how to compute the NAF?

The algorithm for NAF is as follows:

Initialize an empty list.

While n != 0:
    If n is odd:
        If the last digit added is 1, add -1 and set a flag to indicate that the next digit should be 0.
        Else, add 1.
    Else:
        add 0.
    n = n >> 1.

But perhaps it's easier to implement the NAF computation as follows:

public static int[] getNAF(int n) {
    List<Integer> digits = new ArrayList<>();
    while (n != 0) {
        if (n % 2 != 0) {
            digits.add(1);
            n -= 1;
        } else {
            digits.add(0);
        }
        n = n >> 1;
        if (!digits.isEmpty() && digits.get(digits.size() - 1) == 1 && (n % 2 != 0)) {
            digits.set(digits.size() - 1, -1);
            n += 1;
        }
    }
    // Reverse the digits to get the correct order
    Collections.reverse(digits);
    return digits.stream().mapToInt(i -> i).toArray();
}

39 in binary is 100111.

To compute NAF:

We process from the least significant bit.

Initialize:

n = 39.

Step 1: n is odd. So digit is 1. Add to list. n = (39 -1)/2 = 19.

Step 2: n=19 is odd. Previous digit was 1, so set current digit to -1. Add to list. n = (19 +1)/2 = 10.

Step 3: n=10 is even. Add 0. n=5.

Step 4: n=5 is odd. Previous digit is 0, so add 1. n=(5-1)/2=2.

Step 5: n=2 is even. Add 0. n=1.

Step 6: n=1 is odd. Previous digit is 0, add 1. n=0.

So the digits collected are [1, -1, 0, 1, 0, 1].

Initialize an empty list.

    Else:
        add 0.
    n = n >> 1.

But perhaps it's easier to implement the NAF computation as follows:

public static int[] getNAF(int n) {
    List<Integer> digits = new ArrayList<>();
    while (n != 0) {
        if (n % 2 != 0) {
            digits.add(1);
            n -= 1;
        } else {
            digits.add(0);
        }
        n = n >> 1;
        if (!digits.isEmpty() && digits.get(digits.size() - 1) == 1 && (n % 2 != 0)) {
            digits.set(digits.size() - 1, -1);
            n += 1;
        }
    }
    // Reverse the digits to get the correct order
    Collections.reverse(digits);
    return digits.stream().mapToInt(i -> i).toArray();
}

Step 3: n=10 is even. Add 0. n=5.

Step 4: n=5 is odd. Previous digit is 0, so add 1. n=(5-1)/2=2.

Step 5: n=2 is even. Add 0. n=1.

Step 6: n=1 is odd. Previous digit is 0, add 1. n=0.

So the digits collected are [1, -1, 0, 1, 0, 1].
